passage: The ornithologist had spent fifteen years cataloguing migratory bird species along the Atlantic Flyway her findings challenged the long-held assumption that stopover sites south of the Mason-Dixon Line were less ecologically critical than those in the northern breeding grounds.
question: Which choice correctly fixes the sentence boundary error in the passage?

choices.A: The ornithologist had spent fifteen years cataloguing migratory bird species along the Atlantic Flyway; her findings challenged the long-held assumption that stopover sites south of the Mason-Dixon Line were less ecologically critical than those in the northern breeding grounds.
choices.B: The ornithologist had spent fifteen years cataloguing migratory bird species along the Atlantic Flyway, her findings challenged the long-held assumption that stopover sites south of the Mason-Dixon Line were less ecologically critical than those in the northern breeding grounds.
choices.C: The ornithologist had spent fifteen years cataloguing migratory bird species along the Atlantic Flyway: her findings challenged the long-held assumption that stopover sites south of the Mason-Dixon Line were less ecologically critical.
choices.D: The ornithologist, having spent fifteen years cataloguing migratory bird species along the Atlantic Flyway, challenged the long-held assumption that stopover sites south of the Mason-Dixon Line were less ecologically critical than those in the northern breeding grounds.

explanation: The passage is a fused sentence—two independent clauses with no punctuation between them. 'The ornithologist had spent fifteen years cataloguing migratory bird species along the Atlantic Flyway' and 'her findings challenged the long-held assumption...' are both complete independent clauses. Choice A correctly inserts a semicolon between them—the two clauses are causally related, making the semicolon an appropriate connector. Choice B creates a comma splice. Choice C uses a colon—the second clause is a result or implication of the fieldwork rather than a direct definition or explanation of 'Atlantic Flyway cataloguing work'; a semicolon is more precise. Choice D is a valid structural rewrite but makes a more substantial change.
